mcqs.question: Which principle of experimentation involves performing the same test under identical conditions multiple times to ensure consistency?

options.text: Randomization
Replication
Blinding
Control grouping

mcqs.explanation: Replication is the practice of repeating an experiment under the same conditions multiple times to ensure the results are consistent and reliable, rather than arising from random chance.

mcqs.question: In a double-blind experiment, who is unaware of the group assignments?

options.text: Only the subjects
Only the experimenters
Both the subjects and the experimenters
Neither the subjects nor the experimenters

mcqs.explanation: In double-blinding, both the subjects and the experimenters are kept unaware of which group each subject belongs to. This prevents bias from both sides and produces more objective results.

mcqs.question: A two-way factorial design differs from a one-way factorial design because it:

options.text: Uses twice as many subjects
Studies two factors simultaneously and their interaction effects
Requires double-blinding
Performs the experiment twice for replication

mcqs.explanation: A two-way factorial design examines two independent variables (factors) at the same time and also analyzes whether these factors interact with each other to produce combined effects, unlike a one-way design which studies only one factor.

mcqs.question: When comparing the performance of four sorting algorithms using ANOVA, a post-hoc test like Tukey's HSD is used to:

options.text: Determine if any significant difference exists among the groups
Identify which specific pairs of algorithms differ significantly
Randomize the assignment of datasets to algorithms
Replicate the experiment across multiple datasets

mcqs.explanation: ANOVA tells us whether there is a statistically significant difference among group means overall, but it does not specify which pairs differ. Post-hoc tests like Tukey's HSD are then used to identify exactly which specific pairs of groups have significant differences.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of including a control group in an experiment?

options.text: To increase the sample size of the experiment
To provide a baseline for comparison against experimental treatments
To ensure randomization is properly applied
To blind the experimenters from knowing the treatment groups

mcqs.explanation: A control group serves as a baseline that receives no treatment or a standard treatment. By comparing the results of the experimental group against the control group, researchers can determine whether the experimental treatment had a genuine effect beyond what would occur naturally.
